completeness.rationale: Extracted ~5,000 tasks, description vs title search distinction, v2.3 regression, ~2 weeks timing, 10-15 second duration, and long descriptions. Missed CPU spike detail, but that wasn't volunteered by the reporter in this conversation. Got 6 of 7 expected items, with the missing one (CPU spikes) being information the reporter never mentioned.

accuracy.rationale: The root cause hypothesis is remarkably accurate — it lists the actual cause (FTS index dropped/not migrated, query changed to sequential scan/LIKE) as its top two hypotheses. The description-specific vs title-specific distinction is correctly identified. All facts in the summary match the ground truth.

efficiency.rationale: Only 2 questions asked, both essential and well-targeted. The first question established the timeline and dataset size. The second question distinguished description vs title search, which was the key diagnostic differentiator. No wasted turns.

question_quality.rationale: Both questions were excellent. The first combined temporal correlation with dataset size in a single turn. The second asked about search type differentiation (keyword vs tags vs filters) and caching behavior (first vs subsequent), which directly elicited the critical description-vs-title distinction. The reporter even said 'Good question' — the questions demonstrated genuine diagnostic reasoning.

actionability.rationale: The summary provides a precise root cause hypothesis (FTS index issue in v2.3 migration), specific reproduction steps, a recommended fix (diff v2.2 vs v2.3 search implementation, check migration scripts for index changes), and a concrete test case with thresholds. A developer could immediately start investigating the v2.3 migration and search query paths.

notable_strengths: Exceptional efficiency — extracted nearly all critical information in just 2 turns
Root cause hypothesis accurately identified the FTS index regression as the most likely cause
Questions were compounding — each built on the previous answer to narrow the diagnosis
The summary is well-structured with reproduction steps, recommended fix, and a concrete test case
Correctly identified the description-field-specific nature of the regression
notable_weaknesses: Did not collect OS/hardware details (though the summary correctly notes this is unlikely to be root cause)
Did not ask about CPU/memory behavior during slow searches, which would have further confirmed the full-table-scan hypothesis
most_insightful_question: Does the slowness happen on every search, or only certain kinds? For instance, is it slow when you search by keyword, when you filter by tags/labels/status, or both?
missed_opportunities: Could have asked about CPU/memory usage during slow searches to gather evidence for the full-table-scan hypothesis
Could have asked whether the reporter tried reindexing or clearing cache, which would help confirm the index-related root cause
